title: Nonparametric discrete-time survival expert converted to next-lap hazard
summary: Discrete-time survival modeling lets any binary learner model conditional event risk across time bins, and survival forests/boosted survival trees provide a different inductive bias from standard binary trees.
rationale: Discrete-time survival modeling lets any binary learner model conditional event risk across time bins, and survival forests/boosted survival trees provide a different inductive bias from standard binary trees. That gives a low-correlation expert: predict time-to-next-pit with censoring at race end or stint end, then extract the implied hazard for the next lap instead of training only a direct one-step classifier.
implementation_hint: Reconstruct time-to-next-pit for train rows, censor rows with no later stop in race, and fit a discrete-time survival forest or survival boosting model on person-period style data or directly with survival libraries. Convert the estimated survival curve to next-lap hazard and blend this expert with the current XGB/Cat stack by rank or logit.
expected_effect: Probably modest alone, but attractive as a decorrelated expert for ensemble gains and for better late-stint behavior.
risk: This is computationally heavier and can underperform if label reconstruction is noisy or if person-period expansion is not carefully validated by grouped race splits.
